title: Upper Bound in a Sorted Array

content.problemStatement:
You are given a sorted array of integers numsnumsnums of size nnn. Your task is to find the **upper bound** of an integer xxx.

The **upper bound** of xxx is the **smallest index** i such that **nums[i] > x**. If no such index exists, return nnn (the size of the array).

Function DeclarationFunction NameupperBoundupperBoundupperBound – This function finds the upper bound index of a given value in a sorted array.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : A reference to a sorted array of integers.
xxx : The integer value whose upper bound is to be found.
Return Value
Returns the smallest index iii such that nums[i]>xnums[i] > xnums[i]>x
Returns nnn if no such index exists
The answers are taken in 0-based index. So return the value accordingly.
Constraints
1≤n≤1051 \leq n \leq 10^51≤n≤105
−105<nums[i],x<105-10^5 < nums[i], x < 10^5−105<nums[i],x<105
numsnumsnums is sorted in **ascending order**

Input Format

One line containing an integer nnn
One line containing nnn space-separated integers — the sorted array
One line containing an integer xxx


Output Format

Print a single integer — the upper bound index

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The first element greater than 6 is 7 at index 3.

Explanation:
The first element greater than 4 is 6 at index 4.

solution.approach:
We use a **modified binary search**:



Initialize `low = 0`, `high = n`.




While `low < high`:




Compute `mid = (low + high) / 2`.




If `nums[mid] <= x`, move right → `low = mid + 1`.




Else, move left → `high = mid`.






After the loop, `low` will be the index of the first element greater than `x`.




If no such element exists, `low == n`.



Complexity Analysis**Time Complexity**: `O(log n)` (binary search).

**Space Complexity**: `O(1)`` (no extra storage needed).
